# Last Time

::: {.incremental}
+ **Conditional Statements**
+ **The Conditional Operator**
+ **The switch Statement**
+ **Nested if Statements**
+ **Compound Conditions**
+ **Range Checking**
:::

::: notes
Recall that conditionals used true/false questions to choose whether code should run. Today uses the same condition idea, but the control flow can return to an earlier point and repeat.
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

# Today's Agenda

- Why Iteration Matters
- Loop Anatomy and Program Control
- The while Loop
- The do-while Loop
- The for Loop
- Choosing a Loop

::: notes

:::

<!-- Slide 2 -->

{{< include topic_1_why_iteration.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_2_loop_anatomy.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_3_while_loop.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_4_do_while_loop.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_5_for_loop.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_6_choosing_loop.qmd >}}

---

# Key Takeaways

- Iteration is controlled repetition.
- Loops extend the IPO model by repeating part of the process.
- `while` tests before the body; `do-while` tests after the body.
- `for` is best for counted repetition.
- Range-based `for` remains the clearest choice when each collection item is enough.

<!-- Slide 58 -->

---

# Next Time

<!-- Slide 59 -->

---

# Appendix

<!-- Slide 60 -->

---

## Why Iteration Matters: Good Practices

- Name the repeated work before choosing syntax.
- Identify what changes each pass.
- State the condition that should eventually stop the repetition.

<!-- Slide 61 -->

---

## Why Iteration Matters: Common Mistakes

- Starting with loop syntax before identifying the repeated task.
- Repeating code by copying and pasting instead of using a loop.
- Forgetting that the loop needs a stopping condition.

<!-- Slide 62 -->

---

## Why Iteration Matters: More Examples

- Display every item in a list.
- Ask for input until the user gives an acceptable value.
- Calculate one row of output for each hour, day, or record.

<!-- Slide 63 -->

---

## Why Iteration Matters: Practice Problems

- Identify the repeated work in a receipt-printing program.
- Describe what changes each pass when processing test scores.
- Decide whether output should appear each pass or after the loop.

<!-- Slide 64 -->

---

## Why Iteration Matters: AI Search Terms

- C++ iteration design
- C++ loop repetition condition
- C++ IPO model loops
- C++ loop control variable

<!-- Slide 65 -->

---

## Why Iteration Matters: Questions for Reflection

- What work is the program repeating?
- What value or state changes each pass?
- How will the program know the repetition is done?

<!-- Slide 66 -->

---

## Loop Anatomy and Program Control: Good Practices

- Keep the condition readable.
- Update the loop-control value in a predictable place.
- Trace a few passes before trusting the loop.

<!-- Slide 67 -->

---

## Loop Anatomy and Program Control: Common Mistakes

- Writing a condition that never becomes false.
- Updating the wrong variable.
- Confusing the loop body with the code that runs after the loop.

<!-- Slide 68 -->

---

## Loop Anatomy and Program Control: More Examples

```cpp
int count = 1;
while (count <= 5) {
    cout << count << endl;
    count++;
}
```

<!-- Slide 69 -->

---

## Loop Anatomy and Program Control: Practice Problems

- Circle the condition in a loop.
- Identify the loop-control variable.
- Build a Table of Variables for three passes.

<!-- Slide 70 -->

---

## Loop Anatomy and Program Control: AI Search Terms

- C++ loop control flow
- C++ loop control variable
- C++ entry condition loop
- C++ exit condition loop

<!-- Slide 71 -->

---

## Loop Anatomy and Program Control: Questions for Reflection

- Where is the condition checked?
- What sends control back to the top?
- What changes so the loop can eventually stop?

<!-- Slide 72 -->

---

## The while Loop: Good Practices

- Use `while` when zero passes are possible.
- Make the condition describe the reason to continue.
- Update the value that affects the condition.

<!-- Slide 73 -->

---

## The while Loop: Common Mistakes

- Forgetting the update.
- Writing the condition as the reason to stop instead of the reason to continue.
- Assuming the body always runs at least once.

<!-- Slide 74 -->

---

## The while Loop: More Examples

```cpp
int value = 1;

while (value <= 10) {
    cout << value << endl;
    value += 2;
}
```

<!-- Slide 75 -->

---

## The while Loop: Practice Problems

- Write a condition that repeats while `score` is below `70`.
- Trace a loop where `count` starts at `0` and increases by `2`.
- Explain why a `while` body might never run.

<!-- Slide 76 -->

---

## The while Loop: AI Search Terms

- C++ while loop
- C++ while loop trace table
- C++ while loop condition
- C++ infinite loop causes

<!-- Slide 77 -->

---

## The while Loop: Questions for Reflection

- Should the program test before doing the work?
- Could the repeated work be skipped entirely?
- What value moves the loop toward stopping?

<!-- Slide 78 -->

---

## The do-while Loop: Good Practices

- Use `do-while` when one pass is required.
- Keep the repeated prompt or menu inside the body.
- Remember the semicolon after the condition.

<!-- Slide 79 -->

---

## The do-while Loop: Common Mistakes

- Forgetting that the body runs before the test.
- Omitting the final semicolon.
- Using `do-while` when the program should be allowed to skip the body.

<!-- Slide 80 -->

---

## The do-while Loop: More Examples

```cpp
int number;

do {
    cout << "Enter a positive number: ";
    cin >> number;
} while (number <= 0);
```

<!-- Slide 81 -->

---

## The do-while Loop: Practice Problems

- Describe why a menu often uses `do-while`.
- Trace one pass where the user enters `0`.
- Rewrite a simple prompt loop as a `while` loop and compare the setup.

<!-- Slide 82 -->

---

## The do-while Loop: AI Search Terms

- C++ do while loop
- C++ exit condition loop
- C++ do while menu
- C++ do while semicolon

<!-- Slide 83 -->

---

## The do-while Loop: Questions for Reflection

- Does the program need information from the first pass before testing?
- Is one pass always required?
- Would a `while` loop need extra setup before the loop?

<!-- Slide 84 -->

---

## The for Loop: Good Practices

- Use `for` for counted repetition.
- Keep initialization, condition, and update easy to read.
- Use a counter name that describes the count, such as `hour` or `day`.

<!-- Slide 85 -->

---

## The for Loop: Common Mistakes

- Using `<=` when the intended range requires `<`.
- Changing the counter unexpectedly inside the body.
- Using `for` when the number of passes is not known.

<!-- Slide 86 -->

---

## The for Loop: More Examples

```cpp
for (int day = 1; day <= 7; day++) {
    cout << "Day " << day << endl;
}
```

<!-- Slide 87 -->

---

## The for Loop: Practice Problems

- Write a loop that displays numbers 1 through 10.
- Trace a loop that starts at 5 and counts down to 1.
- Decide whether the counter should start at 0 or 1 for a user-facing label.

<!-- Slide 88 -->

---

## The for Loop: AI Search Terms

- C++ for loop
- C++ counted loop
- C++ for loop initialization condition update
- C++ loop counter trace

<!-- Slide 89 -->

---

## The for Loop: Questions for Reflection

- Is the number of passes known?
- What is the first counter value?
- What counter value should stop the loop?

<!-- Slide 90 -->

---

## Choosing a Loop: Good Practices

- Choose based on the repetition need.
- Prefer range-based `for` when the collection controls the loop.
- Prefer the simplest loop that states the control clearly.

<!-- Slide 91 -->

---

## Choosing a Loop: Common Mistakes

- Choosing a loop because it is familiar instead of because it fits.
- Using a counted `for` loop when the condition is not count-based.
- Forgetting that range-based `for` is still available.

<!-- Slide 92 -->

---

## Choosing a Loop: More Examples

| Situation | Loop |
|---|---|
| Every vector element | Range-based `for` |
| Hours 1 through N | `for` |
| Repeat while data exists | `while` |
| Show menu at least once | `do-while` |

<!-- Slide 93 -->

---

## Choosing a Loop: Practice Problems

- Choose a loop for printing one line per student.
- Choose a loop for displaying a menu until quit.
- Choose a loop for processing every value in a vector.

<!-- Slide 94 -->

---

## Choosing a Loop: AI Search Terms

- C++ which loop to use
- C++ while vs do while
- C++ for loop vs range based for
- C++ loop selection decision table

<!-- Slide 95 -->

---

## Choosing a Loop: Questions for Reflection

- What controls the repetition: a collection, a count, a condition, or one required pass?
- Which loop makes that control easiest to see?
- Would another loop require extra setup or awkward logic?

<!-- Slide 96 -->
